objective: Use strategies to compare fractions in real-world problems

pretest.question: Tom ate 2/4 of a pizza. Sara ate 3/4 of the same pizza. Who ate more?
choices: C) They ate the same amount
pretest.hint: The pizzas are the same size. Compare the numerators.

pretest.question: Compare: 1/3 ○ 1/6
pretest.hint: When numerators are the same, the smaller denominator means a bigger piece.

pretest.question: Mia painted 2/6 of a wall. Joe painted 2/3 of the same wall. Who painted more?
choices: C) They painted the same amount
pretest.hint: Same numerator — which denominator makes bigger pieces?

pretest.question: Compare: 4/6 ○ 5/6
pretest.hint: Same denominator. Which numerator is larger?

learn.instruction: When two fractions have the SAME DENOMINATOR, the pieces are the same size. Just compare how many pieces: the bigger numerator is the greater fraction. Example: 3/8 < 5/8 because 3 pieces < 5 pieces.
learn.question: Which is greater: 2/6 or 4/6?

learn.instruction: When two fractions have the SAME NUMERATOR, you have the same number of pieces — but the pieces are different sizes. A smaller denominator means bigger pieces. Example: 2/3 > 2/8 because thirds are bigger than eighths.
learn.question: Which is greater: 1/4 or 1/2?
learn.explanation: Same numerator (1). Halves are bigger than fourths, so 1/2 > 1/4.

learn.instruction: In word problems, first find the two fractions, then decide: same denominator? Compare numerators. Same numerator? Compare denominators. Use >, <, or = to show the result.
learn.question: Amy ran 3/4 of a lap. Ben ran 3/6 of the same lap. Who ran farther?
learn.explanation: Same numerator (3). Fourths are bigger than sixths, so 3/4 > 3/6. Amy ran farther.

try.question: Lily drank 3/8 of a bottle of water. Max drank 6/8 of the same bottle. Who drank more water? Use >, <, or = to compare 3/8 and 6/8.
try.hint: Same denominator. Compare the numerators: 3 vs 6.

try.question: Two gardens are the same size. Rosa planted flowers in 2/3 of her garden. Tom planted flowers in 2/8 of his garden. Who planted more? Compare 2/3 and 2/8.
try.hint: Same numerator. Which denominator means bigger pieces?
guided_steps: Step 3: Thirds are bigger than eighths, so 2/3 > 2/8

practice_r1.question: Jake colored 5/6 of a page. Emma colored 2/6 of the same page. Who colored more?
Two ribbons are the same length. Kim used 3/4 of hers. Liz used 1/4 of hers. Who used more ribbon?

practice_r2.question: Ava read 2/3 of a book. Noah read 2/4 of the same book. Who read more? Explain your thinking.
practice_r2.correct_answer: Ava; 2/3 > 2/4 because same numerator and thirds are larger than fourths.

practice_r2.question: Sam walks 4/6 of a trail. Dan walks 5/6 of the same trail. Who walks farther? Write a comparison using >, <, or =.

practice_r3.question: Three friends share the same-size cake. Ali eats 2/8, Ben eats 5/8, and Cara eats 3/8. Write the fractions in order from least to greatest.
Is 3/4 greater than, less than, or equal to 3/6? Explain how you know WITHOUT drawing a picture.
practice_r3.correct_answer: 3/4 > 3/6. Same numerator (3). Fourths are bigger pieces than sixths because the whole is split into fewer parts. So 3 bigger pieces > 3 smaller pieces.

practice_r3.question: Maya says 1/8 is greater than 1/3 because 8 is greater than 3. Is Maya correct? Explain why or why not.
practice_r3.correct_answer: Maya is NOT correct. A bigger denominator means smaller pieces. 1/8 is a smaller piece than 1/3, so 1/8 < 1/3.
